description: Roman city preserved under Vesuvius eruption of 24 August 79 CE. Continuous urban life from 8th century BCE (Osco-Samnite) to ~79 CE. Rediscovered 1748.
ethical_notes: Human body casts (calchi) are contested: some archaeologists argue they should not be publicly displayed, touching the ethics of ancient human remains. Recent 'House of the Vettii' re-opening (2023) sparked debate over reconstructive restoration vs. preservation as-found.

sources.citation: Beard, M. (2008). Pompeii: The Life of a Roman Town. Profile Books.
UNESCO World Heritage Centre 829

description: Roman seaside town buried under pyroclastic surge from Vesuvius 79 CE. Preserved wooden furniture, food, wax tablets. Villa dei Papiri held the only surviving library from antiquity.
ethical_notes: Boathouse skeletons (~300 found 1980s) revised understanding of eruption timing — most victims died instantly from pyroclastic heat, not ash suffocation. Their display raises same ethics concerns as Pompeii casts.

sources.citation: Wallace-Hadrill, A. (2011). Herculaneum: Past and Future. Frances Lincoln.

description: Neolithic stone circle. Construction phases from 3100 BCE (henge) to 2200 BCE (sarsen trilithons). Alignment with summer solstice. Bluestones transported from Preseli Hills (Wales), ~250km.
ethical_notes: Druid reclaimings since 1970s: English Heritage has granted limited ritual access since 2000. Indigenous British heritage vs tourism tension.

sources.citation: Parker Pearson, M. (2012). Stonehenge: Exploring the Greatest Stone Age Mystery. Simon & Schuster.
UNESCO 373

description: Parthenon (447-432 BCE) under Pericles, architect Iktinos, sculptor Phidias. Temple of Athena Parthenos. Earlier Mycenaean citadel.
ethical_notes: ETHICS-009: Parthenon Marbles (Elgin Marbles) removed by Lord Elgin 1801-1812 and sold to British Museum 1816 — Greek government demands return as cultural repatriation case (UNESCO mediation 1983-). British Museum refuses. Case now exemplary of colonial-era plunder debate.

sources.citation: Hurwit, J. M. (1999). The Athenian Acropolis: History, Mythology, and Archaeology.
St Clair, W. (1998). Lord Elgin and the Marbles. Oxford.

description: Panhellenic sanctuary of Apollo with Oracle. Omphalos ('navel of the world' in Greek cosmology). Pythian Games every 4 years. Closed by Theodosius I's ban on pagan cults 390 CE.

sources.citation: Scott, M. (2014). Delphi: A History of the Center of the Ancient World. Princeton.

description: Sanctuary of Zeus. Olympic Games every 4 years 776 BCE - 393 CE (1169 years). Phidias' chryselephantine Zeus statue (one of Seven Wonders). Abolished by Theodosius I.

sources.citation: Miller, S. G. (2004). Ancient Greek Athletics. Yale.

description: Mycenaean citadel with cyclopean walls. Lion Gate (~1250 BCE). Grave Circles A & B (Heinrich Schliemann excavation 1876). Center of Bronze Age power 1600-1100 BCE before Dorian collapse.
ethical_notes: Schliemann's 'Mask of Agamemnon' is disputed — dating and authenticity under ongoing scholarly debate. Famous unprovenanced excavation practices 1870s.

sources.citation: Wardle, K. A. (1977). Mycenae: An Archaeological History and Guide.

description: Flavian Amphitheatre. Construction 70-80 CE under Vespasian and Titus. ~50,000 spectators. Gladiatorial games, venationes, naval battles (naumachiae). Final gladiator show ~435 CE; last venationes 523 CE.
ethical_notes: Estimated 400,000-1,000,000 deaths over 400 years (gladiators + condemned criminals + animals). Modern use as tourist monument glosses over violence. Pope Benedict XIV consecrated 1749 to Christian martyrs — conversion may be embellished (few documented Christian martyrdoms specifically there).

sources.citation: Hopkins, K., & Beard, M. (2005). The Colosseum. Harvard.

description: Hindu-Buddhist temple complex, Khmer Empire under Suryavarman II (1113-1150). Largest religious monument globally. Originally dedicated to Vishnu; converted to Theravada Buddhist ~14th century. Bas-reliefs depict Mahabharata + Ramayana.
ethical_notes: Khmer Rouge era (1975-1979): civil war damage, looting. Ongoing illicit antiquities trade: Douglas Latchford case (2012-2021, restitution of stolen artifacts to Cambodia).

sources.citation: Higham, C. (2001). The Civilization of Angkor. Phoenix.

description: Mahayana Buddhist stupa, Shailendra dynasty, Central Java. 9 stacked platforms (6 square, 3 circular) with 2,672 relief panels and 504 Buddha statues. Abandoned 14th century after Java's Islamization; rediscovered 1814 by Stamford Raffles.
ethical_notes: 2010 Mount Merapi eruption damaged surface; UNESCO/Indonesia restoration 2011-2015. Climbing restrictions 2020 (wear damage) controversial with local economy dependent on tourism.

sources.citation: Miksic, J. (1990). Borobudur: Golden Tales of the Buddhas. Periplus.

description: Hindu temple complex, Sanjaya dynasty. Trimurti dedication (Shiva, Vishnu, Brahma). 240 temples originally; 8 main + 240 secondary survived in various states. Largest Hindu temple in Indonesia. Ramayana reliefs.

sources.citation: Soekmono (1995). The Javanese Candi: Function and Meaning. Brill.

description: Great Wall system. Qin unification of prior walls (221-206 BCE) under Shi Huangdi. Major Han extension. Most extant sections are Ming (1368-1644). Estimated length 21,196 km (Ming only: 8,851 km). Coordinates for Badaling section.
ethical_notes: Corvée and forced labor deaths over 2000+ years of construction estimated in hundreds of thousands ("longest cemetery on earth"). Memorialized in legend of Meng Jiangnu.

sources.citation: Waldron, A. (1990). The Great Wall of China: From History to Myth. Cambridge.

description: Mogao Caves (Dunhuang), Silk Road oasis. 492 caves with 45,000 m² Buddhist murals + 2,400 statues. Cave 17 (Library Cave) sealed ~1000 CE, discovered 1900 — Aurel Stein & Paul Pelliot removed tens of thousands of manuscripts (1907-1908).
ethical_notes: ETHICS-009 analog: Stein / Pelliot / Langdon Warner removals 1907-1924 = cultural plunder case. ~10,000+ Dunhuang manuscripts in British Museum/BNF/Bibliothèque nationale de France. Chinese government demands return; IDP (International Dunhuang Project) since 1994 digitizes but does not restitute.

sources.citation: Whitfield, S. (2015). Life Along the Silk Road. University of California Press.

description: White marble mausoleum for Mumtaz Mahal, wife of Mughal emperor Shah Jahan. Architect Ustad Ahmad Lahauri. ~20,000 laborers, 1,000 elephants. Symmetric gardens (charbagh). Pietra dura inlays.
ethical_notes: Legend that Shah Jahan had architects' hands severed lacks historical documentation (cf. Ebba Koch). However, labor conditions under Mughal corvée system well-documented as exploitative. Modern air pollution (Agra) causing marble yellowing: restoration ongoing.

sources.citation: Koch, E. (2006). The Complete Taj Mahal. Thames & Hudson.

description: Nabataean capital, rock-cut architecture. Founded ~4th century BCE; became client kingdom of Rome (106 CE annexation by Trajan). Earthquake 363 CE damaged water system, decline begins. Abandoned after 663 CE Arab conquest.
ethical_notes: Bedouin (B'doul) displacement 1985 when area became WHS — relocated to purpose-built village Umm Sayhoun. Ongoing rights conflict with tourism authorities (controlled access, no nomadic use).

sources.citation: Bowersock, G. W. (1983). Roman Arabia. Harvard.

description: Ceremonial capital of Achaemenid Empire. Construction under Darius I (518 BCE), extended by Xerxes I and Artaxerxes I. Destroyed by Alexander the Great 330 BCE — debate persists whether accidental or retaliation for Persian burning of Athens (480 BCE).
ethical_notes: Alexander's fire: Plutarch reports it as courtesan Thaïs's drunken suggestion — historians divided on deliberacy. Persepolis Administrative Archives (30,000+ tablets) at Oriental Institute Chicago since 1930s — Iranian restitution demands ongoing.

sources.citation: Briant, P. (2002). From Cyrus to Alexander. Eisenbrauns.

description: Mesopotamian city. Old Babylonian (Hammurabi, 1792-1750 BCE) + Neo-Babylonian (Nebuchadnezzar II, 605-562 BCE) peaks. Ishtar Gate, Hanging Gardens (Wonder of World, location disputed). Fell to Cyrus 539 BCE. Abandoned after Abbasid period.
ethical_notes: Saddam Hussein palace on ancient site (1980s) damaged archaeology. US/Polish military base 2003-2004 further damage: bulldozing of Nebuchadnezzar bricks, aircraft vibration damage. Ongoing ISIS-era concerns (though Babylon not specifically destroyed). Hanging Gardens location still contested — may have been Nineveh.

sources.citation: Oates, J. (1979). Babylon. Thames & Hudson.

description: Great Pyramid of Khufu (2560 BCE), Pyramid of Khafre, Pyramid of Menkaure + Great Sphinx. Only remaining Wonder of the Ancient World. Khufu's pyramid: 146.6m original (138.5m current), 2.3 million limestone blocks.
ethical_notes: Herodotus claimed 100,000 slaves built Khufu's pyramid — archaeology (Lehner 1988, worker village) contradicts: seasonal paid laborers, not slaves. Ongoing Chinese/alt-archaeology myths (aliens, lost advanced civilizations) dismissed by mainstream.

sources.citation: Lehner, M. (1997). The Complete Pyramids. Thames & Hudson.

description: Trans-Saharan trade center and Islamic scholarly hub under Mali and Songhai Empires. Djinguereber Mosque (1327, Mali period), Sankore Mosque (mid-14th c., became university). ~700,000 manuscripts preserved.
ethical_notes: 2012 Ansar Dine/AQIM destruction: 14 of 16 earthen mausoleums razed (Sankore saints), partial Djinguereber damage. UNESCO in-danger list 2012-2017. Manuscripts evacuation (Timbuktu Renaissance): 350,000+ saved by Abdel Kader Haidara's network. ICC conviction of al-Mahdi 2016 for destruction as war crime (first such conviction).

sources.citation: Hunwick, J. (1999). Timbuktu and the Songhay Empire. Brill.
ICC. (2016). Prosecutor v. Al Mahdi — Judgment ICC-01/12-01/15.

description: Great Zimbabwe — capital of Karanga kingdom, 1100-1450. 'Great Enclosure' with 11m dry-stone walls (no mortar). Population ~18,000 at peak. Abandoned 15th c., environmental or political causes debated.
ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Cecil Rhodes era (1890s+) actively denied African construction — promoted Phoenician/Sabaean myth. Archaeologist Gertrude Caton Thompson (1929) proved Bantu origin; colonial Rhodesia government banned her conclusions. Black Zimbabwe activists adopted name at 1980 independence as direct repudiation.

sources.citation: Garlake, P. (1973). Great Zimbabwe. Thames & Hudson.
Pikirayi, I. (2001). The Zimbabwe Culture. AltaMira.

description: Aksumite Empire capital, monolithic stelae (tallest: 33m Aksum Obelisk, 4th c.). Church of Our Lady of Zion claimed repository of Ark of the Covenant. Ge'ez inscriptions trilingual.
ethical_notes: Aksum Obelisk: Mussolini's Italy removed 1937 to Rome; returned 2005 after 70-year diplomatic struggle. Became precedent for colonial-era restitution cases globally.

sources.citation: Phillipson, D. W. (2012). Foundations of an African Civilisation. James Currey.

description: 11 monolithic rock-hewn churches carved from single volcanic tuff blocks, under King Lalibela (Zagwe dynasty). Called 'New Jerusalem' (pilgrimage destination after Saladin's 1187 Jerusalem capture). Bet Giyorgis church iconic.
ethical_notes: 2021 Tigray War: churches threatened by combat (Ethiopian troops + TPLF + Eritrean forces in region). UNESCO assessment mission delayed by security. Ongoing erosion from rain penetration in single-rock structure — restoration controversial (modern materials vs original).

sources.citation: Phillipson, D. W. (2009). Ancient Churches of Ethiopia. Yale.

description: Phoenician founding (traditional 814 BCE by Queen Dido/Elissa). Mediterranean superpower until Third Punic War 146 BCE — Roman destruction under Scipio Aemilianus. Rebuilt by Caesar 44 BCE as Roman colony. Arab conquest 698 CE destroyed it again.
ethical_notes: 'Salt the earth' legend of Scipio's salt-sowing post-146 BCE is medieval invention (first documented 1863, no ancient sources). Modern Tunis suburbs encroaching on archaeological reserve.

sources.citation: Miles, R. (2010). Carthage Must Be Destroyed. Penguin.

description: Inca (Tawantinsuyu) royal estate of Pachacuti (1450s). 2,430m elevation. 200+ structures: temples, houses, terraces, irrigation. Abandoned during Spanish conquest ~1572, but not looted — Spanish never found it. Rediscovered 1911 by Hiram Bingham.
ethical_notes: ETHICS-009: Hiram Bingham/Yale University removed ~40,000 artifacts 1911-1915. Yale returned them 2011-2012 after century of Peruvian demands. Modern over-tourism (~1.5M visitors/year pre-COVID) damaging pathways; access quotas since 2017.

sources.citation: Burger, R. L. & Salazar, L. C. (2004). Machu Picchu: Unveiling the Mystery of the Incas. Yale.

description: Postclassic Maya city. El Castillo (Temple of Kukulcán) with equinox serpent shadow phenomenon. Great Ball Court largest in Mesoamerica. Sacred Cenote sacrificial context. Itza Maya-Toltec hybrid style, suggesting Toltec influence from Tula.
ethical_notes: Climbing ban 2006 after tourist deaths. Sacred Cenote dredging 1904-1910 by Edward Thompson removed thousands of artifacts to Peabody Museum/Harvard — partial restitution 1959. Contemporary Yucatec Maya not consulted in management historically.

sources.citation: Coggins, C. C. (1992). Artifacts from the Cenote of Sacrifice, Chichen Itza. Peabody.

description: Classic Maya Yax Mutal (modern Tikal). Rivalry with Kaanul (Calakmul) dominated Maya Classic politics. 5 main pyramids, Temple IV 70m. Abandoned ~900 CE during Classic Maya collapse. Glyphic records document 800+ years of dynasty.
ethical_notes: Jaguar filmed here (classic colonial exotic trope). Contemporary Maya (Q'eqchi', Mopán) populations in Petén displaced by 20th c. ladinization and civil war (1960-1996). Park boundaries often in tension with Maya land claims.

sources.citation: Martin, S. & Grube, N. (2000). Chronicle of the Maya Kings and Queens. Thames & Hudson.

description: 'Place where gods were made' (Nahuatl name — Teotihuacanos' self-name unknown). Pyramid of the Sun (3rd largest globally), Pyramid of the Moon, Avenue of the Dead, Temple of Quetzalcoatl. Peak population 125,000-150,000. Collapsed ~550 CE, partially burned.
ethical_notes: Original Teotihuacano ethnicity debated (Otomí? Totonac? Nahua-predecessor?). Aztecs (1300s) found it ruined and named it mythologically. 'Feathered Serpent Pyramid' showed 200+ sacrificial burials — displayed at Museo Nacional de Antropología Mexico.

sources.citation: Cowgill, G. L. (2015). Ancient Teotihuacan. Cambridge.

description: Classic Maya Lakamha' (modern Palenque). Peak under Kʼinich Janaabʼ Pakal I (615-683, tomb discovered 1952 by Alberto Ruz Lhuillier). Temple of the Inscriptions, Palace Tower, hieroglyphic history one of most complete.
ethical_notes: Pakal's jade death mask + sarcophagus cover basis for 'Ancient Astronauts' pseudo-archaeology (Erich von Däniken), rejected by all Mayanists. Contemporary Chol Maya population has cultural continuity claims not always acknowledged in tourism framing.

sources.citation: Ruz Lhuillier, A. (1973). El Templo de las Inscripciones, Palenque. INAH.

description: Capital of Chimor (Chimú Empire), largest adobe city globally (~20 km²). Population 40,000-60,000 at peak. 9 royal citadels (ciudadelas) each for a Chimú king. Absorbed by Inca under Tupac Inca Yupanqui ~1470.
ethical_notes: El Niño flooding 1983, 1998, 2017 severely damaged unglazed adobe. UNESCO In Danger list since 1986. Ongoing illegal adobe removal by local population for house construction — poverty vs heritage conflict.

sources.citation: Moore, J. D. (1996). Architecture and Power in the Ancient Andes. Cambridge.

description: Easter Island moai culture. Nearly 1,000 monolithic statues (tuff from Rano Raraku quarry). Peak population 15,000+ ~1500, collapse to <3,000 by 1722. Ahu ceremonial platforms. Matakiterangi birdman cult post-collapse.
ethical_notes: 1862-1863 Peruvian slave raids captured ~1,500 Rapa Nui (half the population) — smallpox devastation on return. Hoa Hakananai'a moai: British Museum 1868 (HMS Topaze Commander Powell). Rapa Nui Council 2018 formal restitution demand — UK refuses. Chilean annexation 1888 not consented.

sources.citation: Hunt, T. & Lipo, C. (2011). The Statues That Walked. Free Press.

description: Sacred monolith for Aṉangu people (Pitjantjatjara & Yankunytjatjara). Tjukurpa (traditional law) identifies specific rock features as ancestral beings. Continuous Aboriginal custodianship 30,000+ years (oldest dated rock art 10,000 years).
ethical_notes: ETHICS-009: colonial name 'Ayers Rock' imposed 1873 (William Gosse, after S.A. Premier Henry Ayers). Dual-name policy 1993. Climbing banned 26 October 2019 (anniversary of handback to Aṉangu 1985) — returning full control. Uluṟu Climb Ban became global case of Indigenous self-determination over sacred site.

sources.citation: Mountford, C. P. (1965). Ayers Rock: Its People, Their Beliefs, and Their Art. Rigby.
Uluṟu-Kata Tjuṯa National Park. (2019). Climb Closure Announcement.

description: Pre-Pottery Neolithic sanctuary. Oldest known megalithic temple complex (11,500 years before present). T-shaped limestone pillars (up to 5.5m) with carved reliefs of animals. Pre-agriculture — challenges 'Neolithic Revolution' sequencing (religion before farming?).
ethical_notes: Discovery by Klaus Schmidt (German) 1994: revolutionary. Post-2019 'Tas Tepeler' project expanded to 12 sites in region. Pseudoarchaeology claims (Hancock, aliens, 'lost civilizations') rejected by Schmidt and successors. Syrian border proximity: security concerns 2012-2017.

sources.citation: Schmidt, K. (2006). Sie bauten die ersten Tempel. C.H. Beck.

description: Arg-e Bam — largest adobe citadel globally, Silk Road hub. Pre-Achaemenid foundations, peak Parthian/Sasanian period, continuous use to 19th c. Partially destroyed by 26 December 2003 earthquake (~26,000 deaths in Bam city).
ethical_notes: 2003 earthquake destroyed ~80% of citadel + 26,000 human lives. International reconstruction 2004+ (Japan, Italy, France, Germany, USA, etc.) — case of post-disaster heritage recovery with traditional techniques.

sources.citation: UNESCO/ICOMOS. (2004). Report on the Bam Earthquake and Post-Disaster Recovery.

description: Karnak Temple Complex (Ipet-isut 'Most Selected of Places'). Luxor East Bank. Dedicated to Amun-Ra. Construction 2100 BCE - 300 BCE (1800 years). Great Hypostyle Hall: 134 columns (6 central 21m). Main cultic center of Thebes.
ethical_notes: Obelisks removed colonially: Vatican (Constantine), Piazza del Popolo/Rome (Augustus), Istanbul (Theodosius). Modern: Place de la Concorde Paris (1833, Luxor temple-gift). Returns demanded by Egypt — mostly unsettled.

sources.citation: Blyth, E. (2006). Karnak: Evolution of a Temple. Routledge.

description: Knossos palace, Minoan civilization. Peak 1700-1450 BCE (New Palace period). Elaborate frescoes (Bull-Leaping). Linear A (undeciphered) + Linear B (Mycenaean Greek, deciphered Ventris 1952). Destroyed by Mycenaean conquest or volcanic tsunami ~1450 BCE. Modern restoration by Arthur Evans (1900+) controversial.
ethical_notes: ETHICS-002 analog: Arthur Evans's 1900+ 'restorations' using concrete + speculative paint overwrote original fabric. Modern archaeology rejects this approach. 'Minoan' name is Evans's invention (from Greek myth of King Minos) — the civilization's self-designation is unknown.

sources.citation: MacGillivray, J. A. (2000). Minotaur: Sir Arthur Evans and the Archaeology of the Minoan Myth.

description: Mauretanian / Roman North African city. Capital of Mauretania Tingitana. Peak 2nd c. CE, decline after Diocletian withdrawal 285. Last major Berber / Christian presence until 5th c. Idrisid capital 788-808 CE. Lisbon earthquake 1755 damaged ruins (Morocco edge).

sources.citation: Mattingly, D. J. (2011). Imperialism, Power, and Identity: Experiencing the Roman Empire. Princeton.

description: Meroë (archaeological area 'Sulaymāniyya' in modern Sudan). Kushite capital after fall of Napata ~590 BCE. ~200 pyramids (smaller/steeper than Egyptian). Independent continuation of Egyptian-style pharaonic state in Nubia. Ended ~350 CE by Aksumite invasion.
ethical_notes: 1834: Giuseppe Ferlini dynamited 40+ Meroitic pyramid tops searching for gold — artifacts now at Berlin Ägyptisches Museum. Sudan demands restitution. Modern Sudan civil war (2023+) threatens site access.

sources.citation: Shinnie, P. L. (1996). Ancient Nubia. Kegan Paul.

description: Ionian Greek city. Temple of Artemis (Wonder of Ancient World, rebuilt 7x). Roman provincial capital of Asia (133 BCE+). 5th c. Council of Ephesus (431 CE Christological). Abandoned after harbor silting ~6th c.

sources.citation: Scherrer, P. (2000). Ephesus: The New Guide.

description: Early Umayyad 'desert castle' — royal bath + audience hall under Caliph al-Walid II. Frescoes (most intact pre-iconoclastic Islamic art) including the 'Six Kings' depicting defeated Byzantine, Visigoth, Sasanian, Abyssinian, Chinese, Indian rulers.
ethical_notes: Rare Islamic figurative art — challenges post-900 CE aniconic consensus. Restoration 2012 (Spanish-Jordanian team) sparked iconoclasm debate in modern Salafist circles.

sources.citation: Fowden, G. (2004). Qusayr 'Amra: Art and the Umayyad Elite in Late Antique Syria. California.

description: Djenné-Djeno (Old Djenné) archaeological mound — oldest known urban settlement in sub-Saharan Africa (c. 250 BCE). Great Mosque (current structure 1907, rebuilt in Sudano-Sahelian style on original site of 13th c. mosque). Trans-Saharan trade hub.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Great Mosque is annually replastered by entire community (crepissage festival) — living heritage. 1996 the Crepissage Festival was recreated by a film crew for a fashion shoot, damaging the mosque. Mali Minister of Culture response banned Westerners from festival.

sources.citation: McIntosh, R. J. (1995). Excavations at Jenné-Jeno. University of California Press.
